pattern: Writing the split's sum difference as twice the left sum minus the whole array's total reveals that its parity never depends on where the split falls -- only on whether the total itself is even or odd.
hints: Express the difference between the left and right sums using only the left sum and the total sum of the whole array.
Twice any integer is always even, so in that expression the only thing that can flip the parity of the difference is the parity of the total sum itself.
If the total sum of the array is even, every single one of the n-1 possible split points produces an even difference; if it's odd, none of them do.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, even total, four of the splits qualify
statement example, odd total, none qualify
statement example, even total, every split qualifies
smallest possible array, two elements, even total
smallest possible array, two elements, odd total
a long run of odd numbers whose count determines the total's parity
mixture of odds and evens summing to an odd total

stress.description: random arrays across the full length and value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 100))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
